title: Surprise Party

jokes.text: Johnny enters to remind the kids to keep Saturday open, then reveals it's Moira's birthday — which David has confused with Mother's Day
jokes.setup: Johnny asks if they know what Saturday is, implying they should remember
jokes.punchline: David confidently insists the birthday is Mother's Day — a hilariously self-centered confusion
jokes.explanation: Classic David Rose self-absorption: he's conflated his mother's birthday with a holiday about mothers, which is both lazy and perfectly in character. The beats are clean and the payoff lands because it reveals character instantly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't think so.

jokes.text: Alexis announces she's going to Chicago with Ted and when Moira asks 'Can I come?' Alexis flatly says 'No.'
jokes.setup: Moira hears about the Chicago trip and, with complete earnestness, asks if she can join her daughter's romantic getaway
jokes.punchline: Alexis's immediate, unapologetic 'No' — and the fact that Moira asked at all
jokes.explanation: Both characters are exposed simultaneously: Moira's obliviousness to appropriate boundaries and Alexis's blunt self-preservation. Economy is excellent — the joke lives entirely in a single word reply.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We could have the party at Roland's house. It's very spacious.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, no. Absolutely not. Have you seen Roland's house? It looks like a Pottery Barn exploded in a Cracker Barrel.

jokes.text: Johnny says 'Maybe Roland's house' as a venue option. David replies: 'Listen to the words coming out of your mouth.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is brainstorming party venues and floats Roland's house as if it were a reasonable suggestion
jokes.punchline: David's withering, incredulous takedown — implying the idea is so absurd Johnny should hear himself
jokes.explanation: David's disdain for Schitt's Creek's limited offerings is perfectly crystallised here. The line is instantly quotable and works as pure character comedy — only David Rose would say this exactly this way. Strong economy and high character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not trying to be cool. I'm just being myself. And myself happens to be totally rad and fly. In fact, I'm the dopest dad in this whole scene, fo' shizzle.

jokes.text: Johnny attempts 'Talk to the hand, son, because the ears are no longer working' — followed by 'Oh, my God. Okay, that's not at all how that works, so...'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to assert authority using a mid-90s slang phrase
jokes.punchline: The phrase is outdated, wrong, and immediately called out — the patriarch's attempt at cool authority collapses instantly
jokes.explanation: The joke is layered: Johnny is both wrong about the phrase and pathetically earnest about using it. David's correction is withering without being cruel. Good character work — Johnny's out-of-touchness is consistent and well-deployed here.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, I'm going to level with you. When I was your age, I was the coolest guy around. I had the leather jacket, the slicked-back hair, I was listening to all the latest music on my Walkman...

jokes.text: Johnny follows up with 'Cancel your trip, Alexis, because the travel agent is out of the office'
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to assert paternal authority using a second metaphor after the 'Talk to the hand' failure
jokes.punchline: The metaphor makes no sense — 'the travel agent is out of the office' is not a dismissive phrase, it's just confusing, which makes it funnier
jokes.explanation: Funnier than the 'talk to the hand' line because the metaphor actively falls apart — it's not even a culturally legible dismissal, it's just gibberish delivered with full confidence. Johnny's dad-authority-fail is escalated perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, he's doing it again.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What is he doing?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He's trying to be cool. He just called my friend a 'homie.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. And he did finger guns.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh God, not the finger guns.

jokes.text: Ted casually mentions he booked a 'Chicago's Best Deep Dish Pizza Tour' for the afternoon
jokes.setup: Ted is describing romantic weekend plans in Chicago
jokes.punchline: The pizza tour — so wholesome, so Ted, so fundamentally un-glamorous compared to what Alexis would want — perfectly encapsulates their class/personality mismatch
jokes.explanation: Pure character comedy. The detail is so specifically Ted — enthusiastic, earnest, slightly dorky — that it lands without needing a punchline. The comedy is in Alexis's forced 'Oh, my God' response.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my god, dad, please stop trying to use slang.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's literally painful to watch. You sound ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What? I'm just trying to be cool. That's totally lit, fam.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Please never say that again.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm experiencing actual secondhand embarrassment right now.

jokes.text: Alexis hears about the pizza tour and immediately pivots: 'Well, that's probably gonna have to change, but what else did you book for us?'
jokes.setup: Ted has just described the pizza tour as a fun plan
jokes.punchline: Alexis has already mentally cancelled it and moves on without apology — total Alexis Rose energy
jokes.explanation: Sharp character work. Alexis doesn't even bother to soften the dismissal — the entitlement is so casual it becomes comedic. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I've planned us a romantic getaway.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, really? Where?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: A pizza tour of the Tristate area.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, a what?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: We'll visit the five best pizzerias, stay in a Quality Inn, and I've made a spreadsheet of optimal driving routes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted, that's not romantic, that's a logistics presentation.

jokes.text: Moira's response to learning about room service: 'Does that mean room service?' then 'Babe!' — her childlike excitement
jokes.setup: Ted mentions they got upgraded at the hotel
jokes.punchline: Moira — a woman who has lived in penthouses and stayed at the finest hotels globally — reacts to potential room service like a child at Christmas, revealing how much the deprivation of Schitt's Creek has recalibrated her expectations
jokes.explanation: Wait — re-reading context, this appears to be Alexis reacting to Ted's hotel upgrade news, not Moira. But the comedy still works as character-specific: someone who's lost luxury reacting with disproportionate joy to room service. Strong character integration regardless of who delivers it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: This conference is just so important. For us.

jokes.text: Ted reveals they're staying at the 'Clarendon Executive Suites and Apartments' — Alexis reacts with 'Babe!' as if it's the most glamorous thing imaginable
jokes.setup: They've established Ted's earnest, un-glamorous taste (pizza tour). Now he reveals the hotel.
jokes.punchline: The hotel name — 'Executive Suites and Apartments' — is clearly mid-tier at best, but Alexis reacts as though it's The Ritz. The gap between expectation and reality is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A decent comic beat built on the ironic gap between Alexis's pampered background and what she's now excited about. The 'Apartments' part of the hotel name does real work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I just assumed, given your... rustic upbringing, that you wouldn't understand the finer points of—

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: David, I will slap you.

jokes.text: David convinces Ted's veterinary conference is in 'a Chicago suburb' — Alexis hadn't told Ted about cancelling
jokes.setup: David ambushes Alexis's conversation with Ted to reveal Alexis hasn't told Ted she's cancelling Chicago
jokes.punchline: David casually drops the bombshell 'Alexis, did you not tell Ted about your change of plans?' in front of Ted, with complete fake innocence
jokes.explanation: David's gleeful betrayal of his sister — framed as helpful concern — is a strong sibling-comedy beat. The cringe comes from Alexis being caught out in front of Ted.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: If this party isn't perfect, it will literally ruin my life.

jokes.text: David tells Alexis she 'can't miss your own mother's surprise party' — then says to Ted 'I'm sure you're from a really solid home and... Understand.'
jokes.setup: David is trying to guilt Alexis into cancelling her Chicago trip and turning to Ted for support
jokes.punchline: The backhanded jab at Ted's presumably normal, functional upbringing — implying Ted can't understand the chaos of the Rose family and therefore his opinion doesn't count
jokes.explanation: David's weaponisation of the Roses' dysfunction — using it to win an argument while simultaneously insulting Ted's normalcy — is a nice bit of character-specific wit. The fragment 'Understand.' as a standalone sentence lands well.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's very kind of you, Roland, but I think we're going to need something a little more... upscale. You know, something with a bit more... finesse.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What do you mean? My place is nice!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it's nice in the way that a gas station bathroom is nice. Functional, but not exactly where you want to spend an evening.

jokes.text: Alexis threatens: 'I'm gonna slap you in the face.'
jokes.setup: David has just been condescending to Ted on Alexis's behalf
jokes.punchline: The casual, matter-of-fact threat of physical violence from Alexis — delivered not in anger but as a logical consequence
jokes.explanation: Alexis's deadpan threat is perfectly economical. The comedy is in the tone — she's not angry, just informative. High economy score because it does everything in six words.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Okay, so here's what we're going to do. You tell them you're my business associate.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Your business associate. Got it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: My name is Johnny Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Right, right. And what's your name?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Johnny Rose. That's... that's what I just said.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, for you. What about for me?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Your name is Roland Schitt.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Okay, and what's my business?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're my associate in my business.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Right, but what kind of business?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Just... just say you work with me. That's all you need to remember.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Got it. I work with... Johnny. And your last name?

jokes.text: David asks Alexis to 'ask Mutt for the barn' because 'I know that you guys have a close working relationship, so...'
jokes.setup: David needs the barn for the party and Alexis has a romantic history with Mutt
jokes.punchline: David's loaded, knowing euphemism 'close working relationship' — implying Alexis should leverage her romantic/sexual past with Mutt as social currency
jokes.explanation: Solid bit of layered implication. David's archness is well-deployed here — he's both strategic and catty in one move.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, we need to discuss the business model for the motel. This isn't working.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's fun time!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, Roland. Fun time is not a business strategy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: But it's fun time though.

jokes.text: 'David, you and Mom literally ruin my life.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has been backed into cancelling her romantic trip and helping with a party she doesn't want to plan
jokes.punchline: The melodramatic but completely sincere 'literally ruin my life' — which, given the Roses' actual life circumstances, is both true and absurd
jokes.explanation: Classic Alexis Rose — the hyperbole lands because she means it completely. It's also darkly ironic given their actual ruined life circumstances. Highly quotable in context.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's not about the cake.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's a torte.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Well, dinner at our place. Problem solved.' — offered as a solution to Moira's fancy birthday venue problem
jokes.setup: Johnny asks Roland for help finding an elegant, fancy venue for the party
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately offers his own house as if it's an obvious luxury solution, completely blind to its inadequacy
jokes.explanation: Callbacks to the earlier joke about Roland's house, now with Roland himself unwittingly confirming the gag. His complete lack of self-awareness is the engine of his comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're not getting a cake.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not a cake, it's a torte.

jokes.text: Johnny suggests to Roland the cover story is 'Jocelyn is throwing her annual charity fundraiser.' Roland replies: 'She hasn't mentioned anything to me about it.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is explaining a fake cover story to Roland
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately interprets the fictional fundraiser as real — his literalism creates instant, perfect confusion
jokes.explanation: This is the setup for the episode's best comedic sequence. Roland's literalism is perfectly in character — he's not stupid, he just processes things in a uniquely literal way. The joke creates the engine for everything that follows. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, absolutely not. I'm not getting murdered on the side of the road by some random person who definitely has a skin suit collection in their basement.

jokes.text: Johnny has to explain: 'It's not a real fundraiser, Roland. It's just a pretend fundraiser to get Moira out of the house.'
jokes.setup: Roland has misunderstood the plan so fundamentally that Johnny must explain the concept of a fake event
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of having to say out loud 'it's just a pretend fundraiser' — the phrase collapses the elegant scheme into something childish
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the deflation — Johnny's sophisticated plan reduced to baby-talk. The line 'it's just a pretend fundraiser' is inherently funny when said by an adult seriously.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not being ridiculous. I have a very specific palate, and I refuse to compromise on cake. There's a difference.

jokes.text: The full 'I know/you know/she doesn't know' logic loop between Johnny and Roland about the birthday
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to explain why Roland can tell Moira about the fake fundraiser despite knowing it's her birthday
jokes.punchline: The explanation becomes a circular, increasingly maddening Abbott-and-Costello loop: 'You only know it's her birthday because I just told you it's her birthday. Moira doesn't know you know it's her birthday.'
jokes.explanation: The episode's comedic peak in the Roland/Johnny plotline. The logic is genuinely circular in a way that mirrors how confused Roland actually is — it's Whose-on-First-style confusion where both men are simultaneously right and wrong. Escalates beautifully, and the structure (Johnny going in circles trying to explain what should be simple) is perfectly constructed. Roland's literalism as a comedy engine is fully realised here.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You're invited to... uh... a thing. At my place. Next... Saturday? Or was it Sunday? One of those days.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It starts at... noon-ish? Maybe evening? Wear something nice. Or casual. Whatever you got.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, and bring a plus one. Or don't. I can't remember if I said that or not.

jokes.text: 'Tell me you understand the plan.' / 'Yes, Johnny, I understand the plan! Jeez! And I don't appreciate being talked to like a child!'
jokes.setup: Johnny, exhausted and unsure Roland understands, asks him to confirm comprehension
jokes.punchline: Roland's indignant reaction — offended at being treated like a child, when he has just demonstrated extraordinary childlike confusion — is perfectly ironic
jokes.explanation: The irony is tight: Roland's outrage at being condescended to lands right after proving he needed every ounce of that condescension. The indignation is funnier because it's completely sincere.

jokes.text: 'So, I am gonna need a lift to Elmdale.' — Roland, immediately after the heated explanation, casually drops a new request
jokes.setup: Johnny and Roland have just barely resolved the tense, confusing fundraiser conversation
jokes.punchline: Roland pivots with zero emotional transition to ask for a personal favour — deflating the whole tense scene
jokes.explanation: The sudden tonal pivot is the joke. Roland's complete emotional reset — from indignant to breezy — is a clean character beat with good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It was for... uh... the thing. You know, the... the sick... the sick children? No wait, the blind... no, no, the deaf. Or was it the... the ones with the legs?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Either way, I wrote a very generous check to whatever it was. Very generous. Five figures.

jokes.text: David asks Stevie for a ride; she says she's working. David: 'Oh, you are?' / Stevie: 'Yes. I have a job that I can't just leave to drive you to Elmdale.'
jokes.setup: David walks in and finds Stevie apparently doing nothing — from his perspective
jokes.punchline: His genuine surprise that she has a job and can't just drop everything for him — pure Rose entitlement meeting Stevie's flat refusal
jokes.explanation: The dynamic between David and Stevie is one of the show's great comic pairings. His oblivious entitlement and her deadpan refusal are both perfectly in character. The 'Oh, you are?' carries an enormous amount of Rose-ness in three words.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, that's very kind of you to offer, but we're really not looking for any additional input at this time.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, I know what you mean. You want to dress them up a little bit, make them look a little less... disabled?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, that's not— no.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I've got some clip-on ties at the store.

jokes.text: Stevie claims she's 'working' while visibly doing nothing, and David's sceptical 'I don't know, nothing?'
jokes.setup: David arrives at the motel desk needing a favour from Stevie
jokes.punchline: Stevie is clearly not doing anything but insists she's working; David's flat 'I don't know, nothing?' is a perfectly calibrated challenge
jokes.explanation: A quick, functional David/Stevie sparring exchange. Their dynamic — mutual low-grade antagonism masking affection — is established efficiently. Not a standout joke but a good baseline scene-setter.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, you can't just take time off from bedding women.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: No, I could do both simultaneously.

jokes.text: David's reason for not hitchhiking: 'And wake up chained to a pole in a trucker's basement? Not today.'
jokes.setup: Stevie suggests David could just hitchhike to Elmdale
jokes.punchline: David's specific, vivid, darkly comic refusal — the trucker's basement is an absurdly detailed fear delivered completely straight
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Chained to a pole in a trucker's basement' is startlingly specific and darkly comic, and 'Not today' as the dismissal is pitch-perfect deadpan. It's quotable out of context, reveals David's dramatic inner life, and lands with perfect economy. Barely misses 8+ on craft only because it's a one-liner without structural complexity.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: You had a bug in your hair.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

jokes.text: Stevie: 'I can't just up and leave because you want cake.'
jokes.setup: David has been building his case for why the torte retrieval is a legitimate emergency
jokes.punchline: Stevie reduces the entire argument to its bare absurdity: 'because you want cake'
jokes.explanation: A callback that also escalates — she deliberately calls it 'cake' again, reinforcing her refusal to validate David's pretension while also nailing the comedy of the situation: this is, stripped down, just a man who wants cake. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Profitable non-profits. That's the dream, isn't it?

jokes.text: David's response: 'Okay. Well, when you put it like that, it makes me sound ridiculous... which I'm not. So, thank you... for your services this afternoon.'
jokes.setup: David has been called out for wanting 'cake' and briefly seems to accept the characterisation
jokes.punchline: He catches himself, denies being ridiculous, then exits with an absurdly formal 'thank you for your services' — a social face-save that makes him seem more ridiculous
jokes.explanation: A beautifully observed character moment. The semi-admission followed by denial followed by weirdly formal exit is quintessential David. The 'which I'm not' is especially good — the protest is itself ridiculous. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Why does everyone seem so nervous around me? I'm just trying to be helpful and fabulous.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you literally told Jocelyn her outfit was 'a cry for help' last week.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was being constructive! How is that intimidating?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You also said her marriage was 'a noble experiment that's clearly failing.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it is. But that's not about me being intimidating, that's just me being honest.

jokes.text: Roland arrives at the café, opening with 'Hey. Hey. Sorry I'm late, Johnny. Had a little memorising to do.'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks 'Late for what, Roland?' — implying Roland has already broken cover
jokes.punchline: Roland has not only arrived 'late' to a casual cafe meeting (revealing he thought of it as a scheduled mission) but has announced his memorisation process, immediately blowing any cover
jokes.explanation: Brilliant. 'Had a little memorising to do' immediately signals to Moira (and Johnny) that something is afoot, and the fact that Johnny has to say 'Late for what?' shows Roland has already derailed. The phrase 'memorising to do' is funny in itself — like he's cramming for an exam. High character integration; only Roland would approach this mission this way.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Are we... are we actually doing this? We're actually planning a fake fundraiser?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, no. This is insane. I can't— I have a reputation in this town. What if people find out?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I'm going to jail. That's where this is going. I'm going to jail, and Roland's going to have to explain to the kids why Mommy's in prison.

jokes.text: Roland's fundraiser pitch: 'an annual... Elegant dress-up event that Jocelyn is hosting. Well, no, I'm not inviting you, but I'm saying we're... We'll see each other there.'
jokes.setup: Roland is supposed to casually drop news of a fundraiser to get Moira to attend
jokes.punchline: The description falls apart in real time — 'dress-up event,' 'well, no, I'm not inviting you' — Roland is actively disassembling the cover story as he delivers it
jokes.explanation: Chris Elliott's delivery (inferred from the text) of a pitch that collapses mid-sentence is masterful cringe comedy. 'Well, no, I'm not inviting you' is an extraordinary self-correction that makes zero sense in context — it both breaks the cover story and confuses Moira. Very high character integration: this exact implosion is only possible from Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Stevie wouldn't drive me because she said something about needing to focus on herself right now, and I was like, okay, but also you literally just sit in a car, so I don't know what that means. Like, is she going through something? Does she have a weird thing with cars? I feel like there's clearly something deeper going on that she's not telling me about.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Well, no, I'm not inviting you, but I'm saying we're... We'll see each other there. At the very big event that Jocelyn is hosting.'
jokes.setup: Roland realises he's accidentally invited Moira and Johnny to the fake fundraiser
jokes.punchline: His self-correction — 'Well, no, I'm not inviting you, but...' — and then the retreat to 'the very big event' makes things substantially worse
jokes.explanation: Roland digging himself deeper. The comedy is in watching someone try to correct a gaffe and create a larger one. 'The very big event' is a funny phrase — vague enough to mean nothing, emphatic enough to seem suspicious.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I asked Stevie to help me, but she said she couldn't because she's going through some stuff right now. And I was like, 'Okay, but what kind of stuff? Like, is it a physical thing? Is it emotional? Is it... situational?' And she just walked away, which I'm pretty sure means it's all three.

jokes.text: Roland apologises to Johnny mid-pitch: 'Oh, sorry, Johnny. Let me start over here.' — Johnny: 'Why are you apologising to me?'
jokes.setup: Roland is mid-pitch to Moira and has begun to implode
jokes.punchline: He turns to apologise to Johnny — completely breaking the fourth wall of the deception — prompting Johnny's horrified correction
jokes.explanation: The breaking of the deception in real time is exquisite cringe comedy. Roland's social instinct is to apologise — but apologising to Johnny mid-pitch to Moira is such a spectacular failure that it becomes its own comedy. Johnny's 'Why are you apologising to me?' captures his horror perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom once intimidated Hillary Clinton at a charity gala.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That didn't happen.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, it didn't. But I wanted to see if you would believe me, because that's the kind of thing she would do.

jokes.text: Roland lands on the cause: 'No, no. It's a fundraiser for the thing with the children that... The... With the mouth... Cleft palates.'
jokes.setup: Roland is trying to name the fictional charity cause
jokes.punchline: The halting, searching description — 'the thing with the children... with the mouth... Cleft palates' — is somehow both incompetent and weirdly touching
jokes.explanation: The verbal stumble toward 'cleft palates' is physically painful in a great way. Roland's brain working in real time, landing on an actual medical condition through pure desperation, is a perfect comedy escalation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, we passed the 'Exit Elmdale' sign? That was our exit.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, we've gone too far. We're never going to find it now.

jokes.text: Roland adds: 'And Joc' does it every year...' — implying a made-up fundraiser has now been established as an annual tradition
jokes.setup: Roland is improvising details about the fake fundraiser
jokes.punchline: Adding 'every year' to a fictional event deepens the absurdity — Jocelyn apparently runs a cleft palate fundraiser annually that Roland has never mentioned to Johnny
jokes.explanation: A quieter beat in the Roland implosion sequence, but the 'every year' detail adds layers of absurdity without needing much setup.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: It's for the... uh... The Society for the Preservation of... Llama Wellness and Ethical Grooming Practices.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'It's hard to look at those kids and all that, I get it.' — Roland, trying to cover for Moira potentially not coming
jokes.setup: Moira says they'd probably just be hanging around the motel on Saturday
jokes.punchline: Roland, ad-libbing, implies Moira doesn't want to attend because it's emotionally difficult to 'look at those kids' — a wildly inappropriate characterisation delivered with warm sincerity
jokes.explanation: Dark, slightly transgressive, and perfectly Roland. He's trying to help but has implied Moira is too emotionally fragile to look at sick children — which is both insulting and darkly funny. The warmth of his delivery makes it weirder.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Wait, if they're free, how is that a fundraiser?

jokes.text: After the chaotic pitch, Roland says: 'I think that went well.'
jokes.setup: Roland has just delivered one of the most catastrophically bad cover stories imaginable
jokes.punchline: His completely sincere assessment that it went well — total self-unawareness as punchline
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the Roland cafe scene. Five words of complete obliviousness deliver an enormous punchline. The comedy works because we've watched the disaster unfold — the gap between what happened and Roland's self-assessment is the whole joke. Very high economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's it. I am taking over this party planning. I'm declaring a coup d'état.

jokes.text: Alexis runs into Mutt while he's working and immediately asks to borrow his barn, adding: 'But if you can't take the night off from bedding women, I totally understand.'
jokes.setup: Alexis needs to ask Mutt a favour but their romantic history makes this awkward
jokes.punchline: She wraps jealousy and a jab at his lifestyle into a performatively magnanimous offer to 'understand'
jokes.explanation: 'Bedding women' as Alexis's casual way of describing Mutt's love life is funny in its archness — it's both jealous and weirdly formal. Good character work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's it. I'm done. I'm quitting.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, come on.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No. I'm counting to three, and if this isn't resolved, I'm walking out that door. One...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Two...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Two and a half...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Two and three-quarters...

jokes.text: Mutt's response to the 'bedding women' comment: 'Well, who says I can't do both?'
jokes.setup: Alexis has offered an out, suggesting he might be too busy with romantic pursuits
jokes.punchline: Mutt's completely confident, borderline cocky suggestion that lending the barn and bedding women are not mutually exclusive
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — seven words, maximum character expression. Mutt's unflappable confidence is funny and a little disarming. The rhythm of the line is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Alexis, I can't believe you're suggesting we steal from the store.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, I already stole the security camera footage from last week, so...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You what?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? I looked really cute in it and I wanted to keep it.

jokes.text: Mutt removes a bug from Alexis's hair. She reacts: 'Oh. Ew!'
jokes.setup: A tender, almost romantic moment between Mutt and Alexis
jokes.punchline: The moment is undercut by the mundane grossness of a bug — Alexis's disgust instantly deflates any romantic tension
jokes.explanation: A standard romantic-tension-deflation beat. The bug is a somewhat predictable device but it's executed crisply. The economy is good — two words from Alexis do the job.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You stole sunglasses? Alexis, you're an adult!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, daddy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Do you? Because you're acting like a child. What's next, are you going to shoplift a toy? Steal candy from a baby?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Sorry doesn't cut it. You need to return those sunglasses, and you need to apologize to the store. And frankly, I'm very disappointed in you.

jokes.text: Moira approaches Jocelyn about the fundraiser she didn't know she was running: 'I hope so, 'cause I'd like to be involved.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is caught off-guard, having only just learned she's supposed to be running a fundraiser
jokes.punchline: Moira wants to join the committee of a fundraiser that doesn't exist — and Jocelyn has to play along
jokes.explanation: The plan is already spiralling — Moira's eagerness to 'be involved' is perfectly ironic given she's the birthday girl. Jocelyn's trapped position creates good cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Wait, there was no fundraiser? But I already planned my outfit!

jokes.text: Moira rattles off her non-profit credentials: 'I was on the board of some of the most profitable non-profits, from San Francisco to London.'
jokes.setup: Moira is establishing her credentials to help with the fundraiser
jokes.punchline: 'The most profitable non-profits' is an oxymoron that Moira delivers with complete conviction — she either doesn't notice or doesn't care
jokes.explanation: 'Profitable non-profits' is a genuinely sharp piece of writing — it's the kind of malapropism that Moira would say and believe. It reveals her as someone who absorbed the glamour of charity work without understanding its mechanics. High character integration and originality.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my stars! I have been positively ambushed by the element of astonishment! My cardiovascular system is engaged in a most vigorous palpitation, and I find myself bereft of my customary eloquence. This unexpected turn of events has quite thoroughly dispatched my composure to the netherregions!

jokes.text: Moira tells Jocelyn: 'Jocelyn, you must stop being intimidated by me. It's unnerving.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn says she didn't want to 'impose' by asking Moira for help
jokes.punchline: Moira interprets this as intimidation — and her solution to Jocelyn's discomfort is to instruct her to stop being intimidated, which is itself intimidating
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Moira lines. The paradox is perfect: she tells Jocelyn to stop being intimidated in the most intimidating way possible. It's also a deeply revealing character moment — Moira genuinely cannot see her own effect on people. High originality, excellent structure (the instruction contradicts itself), and this line could only come from Moira Rose. Fully earns its 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, I want to apologize. I've been a real jerk to you, and I feel terrible about it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, are you getting your period?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What? No!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because that would explain so much.

jokes.text: Johnny's horrified 'Oh, fuck!' upon learning Moira got involved in planning the fundraiser
jokes.setup: David calls Johnny with an update — Moira has enthusiastically joined the fundraising committee
jokes.punchline: Johnny's two-word reaction captures total defeat
jokes.explanation: The joke is almost entirely in the setup — everything we know about Moira makes Johnny's reaction immediately understandable and funny. The economy is near-perfect but the originality is low (it's just a swear reaction beat). Still works well in context.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So what, you're just gonna be weird and short with everyone all day?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not being weird.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You literally just told your brother his hair looked like a dead animal.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it does.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay, so is this a period thing?

jokes.text: David explains the cake situation: 'I think she's going through something right now. I don't know. Something with her body.'
jokes.setup: David is explaining why he didn't get the cake — Stevie refused to drive him
jokes.punchline: His vague, half-sympathetic explanation for Stevie's refusal — 'something with her body' — is both hilariously vague and a total non-answer
jokes.explanation: David's attempt at sympathy is undercut by his complete disinterest in what's actually happening with Stevie. 'Something with her body' is deliberately vague in a funny way — he's giving her a pass while revealing he has no idea what's going on.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Will you dance with me?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: My usual dance partners are a bottle of red wine and my own crippling anxiety.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I asked you to get her invited to the fundraiser, not involved in the fundraiser.'
jokes.setup: David reports that Moira has inserted herself into planning the fake fundraiser
jokes.punchline: Johnny's precise articulation of exactly how the plan went wrong — the distinction between 'invited' and 'involved' — captures the cascading failure
jokes.explanation: The comedy of specificity — Johnny doesn't just say 'this went wrong,' he identifies exactly which failure point matters. The invited/involved distinction is sharp and the repetition works rhythmically.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I usually dance alone in my room with the lights off, and I pretend that nobody can see me.

jokes.text: David: 'You didn't think your mother would get involved in planning a fundraiser?'
jokes.setup: Johnny is blaming the situation on Roland, calling it a bad mistake
jokes.punchline: David flips it back — pointing out that anyone who knows Moira would have anticipated this outcome
jokes.explanation: A good straight-man reversal — David, usually the chaos agent, is the most reasonable person in this exchange. The implication that this was obviously going to happen is funny because it's true.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wow, you look great in that suit. Did you kill someone?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: 'My God, she had Hillary shaking last year at the Clinton Foundation dinner.'
jokes.setup: David is making the case that Moira cannot be controlled in an event planning context
jokes.punchline: The specific claim that Moira Rose had Hillary Clinton 'shaking' is an absurd hyperbole delivered completely straight — implying Moira is more intimidating than a former Secretary of State
jokes.explanation: Excellent piece of world-building comedy. The Rose family's former wealth and connections are delivered as a comic prop here — the idea that Moira Rose's event-planning aggression rattled Hillary Clinton is both absurd and somehow completely believable given what we know of Moira. High originality and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I wore this suit to my cousin's wedding and he met his wife that night. So I'm hoping for the same luck at this party.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Stupid Roland. Roland?' / David: 'How did Roland get involved in this?' — escalating Roland blame
jokes.setup: The plan has gone off the rails and everyone needs someone to blame
jokes.punchline: Roland is the obvious scapegoat and both Roses arrive at his name with equal frustration — even though Johnny is the one who involved him
jokes.explanation: A solid callback beat. The irony of Johnny blaming Roland when he's the one who recruited Roland is good, but it's a relatively mild laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I'm hoping to get lucky again.

jokes.text: 'The North Korean army could not keep my mother under control when it comes to event planning.'
jokes.setup: David tells Johnny to just 'keep your mother under control'
jokes.punchline: Johnny's hyperbolic response — invoking the North Korean army as inadequate to the task — is a perfect escalation of Moira's force of nature quality
jokes.explanation: A strong line. The North Korean army comparison is an absurdist escalation that works because it's a specific, unusual comparator — not just 'no one could stop her' but a specific military force. High quotability and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This is lovely, truly. But I must insist: never do this again. The shock to my system was genuinely traumatic. I nearly fainted, and my heart rate is still irregular. I simply cannot endure another ambush of this magnitude.

jokes.text: Johnny and Alexis in the car — the silent reaction beat when they've missed the Elmdale exit while David was sleeping
jokes.setup: They're en route to pick up the cake. David has been asleep.
jokes.punchline: Inferred: Johnny/David wakes up disoriented, and we discover they've blown past the exit
jokes.explanation: A standard sitcom misadventure beat. Gets the job done without being particularly inventive.

jokes.text: The missed-exit reveal: David slept for '45 maybe' minutes, and the last sign said 'Exit Elmdale' — meaning they've already passed it
jokes.setup: David asks where they are; Johnny says they're 'almost there' and mentions the last sign said 'Exit Elmdale'
jokes.punchline: David has to explain: 'Well, that was the exit to Elmdale' — they've gone too far
jokes.explanation: The role reversal — David being the competent one explaining the navigation error to Johnny — is a nice touch. The comedy is in Johnny's cheerful obliviousness.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'But you were sleeping!' / David: 'Well, wake me up!' / Johnny: 'No! Because you're so grumpy when you wake... Like, you're grumpy right now.'
jokes.setup: They've missed the exit and are arguing about whose fault it is
jokes.punchline: Johnny's perfectly circular defence — he didn't wake David because David is grumpy when woken, which is proven by the fact that David is grumpy right now
jokes.explanation: A lovely bit of circular logic that also doubles as character observation. Johnny's self-defeating argument ('you're grumpy right now') is immediately proven correct by David's reaction to being told he's grumpy. Good structure and high character integration.

jokes.text: Moira: 'The Doctors Who Uncleft The Mouths'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn and David are trying to figure out the name of the charity at the party planning meeting
jokes.punchline: Moira invents 'The Doctors Who Uncleft The Mouths' — a magnificently wrong paraphrase of 'Operation Smile' / cleft palate surgery charities
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. 'The Doctors Who Uncleft The Mouths' is a comedic masterpiece: it callbacks Roland's fumbling 'cleft palates' improvisation, it's linguistically inventive ('uncleft' as a verb), it's delivered with Moira's full confidence as if this is the official name, and it captures exactly how Moira processes information — she absorbed the concept and repackaged it in her own baroque idiom. It's absurdist, perfectly structured as a callback payoff, and almost entirely unique to this character. Comparable in quality to great 8+ anchor examples — this is the kind of line that gets clipped and shared. Justifiably scores highest in the episode.

jokes.text: Moira demands to know the name of the charity: 'Again, what is the organisation that it's for?' / 'The Doctors Who Uncleft The Mouths.'
jokes.setup: Moira is demanding proper event planning details for the fake fundraiser
jokes.punchline: David, completely on the spot, invents 'The Doctors Who Uncleft The Mouths' — an absurd, technically accurate but utterly non-functional name for a cleft palate charity
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and a genuine standout. 'The Doctors Who Uncleft The Mouths' is brilliant because it sounds like someone desperately reverse-engineering a charity name from Roland's invented cause description — it's perfectly grammatically weird (the verb 'uncleft'), completely plausible as something David would say under pressure, and deeply quotable. It also pays off Roland's 'cleft palates' moment elegantly. High originality, strong character integration, excellent quotability.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Never heard of it.' — after hearing the charity name (which she just invented)
jokes.setup: Moira has just coined 'The Doctors Who Uncleft The Mouths' as the charity name
jokes.punchline: She immediately disavows knowledge of the organisation she just named — complete self-unawareness in real time
jokes.explanation: Immediately follows joke #48 and doubles the laugh. Moira invents a charity name and then says she's never heard of it in the same breath — the self-contradiction is so rapid it becomes its own comedy layer. The deadpan delivery implied by the text makes this feel like a throwaway that's actually been precisely engineered. Very high scores across the board.

jokes.text: Moira asks: 'And who are the trustees?' David: 'I don't think they're gonna come.' Moira: 'Oh, how very churlish of them.'
jokes.setup: Moira is interrogating the legitimacy of the fake fundraiser's governance
jokes.punchline: 'Churlish' — Moira's vocabulary is so elevated that her disapproval of fictional trustees not attending a fictional event is delivered with aristocratic condescension
jokes.explanation: 'Churlish' is a perfect Moira word — it's both archaic and condescending. The comedy is in her complete investment in the logistics of a nonexistent event.

jokes.text: Moira: 'How much are tickets?' / David: 'Um, free?' / Moira: 'Oh, my God! Do you people know what the word fundraising means?'
jokes.setup: The party planners haven't figured out ticket prices for the fictional charity event
jokes.punchline: Moira's outrage at 'free' tickets — completely oblivious to the fact that this is a fake event for her own birthday party
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed irony. Moira is furious that no one is charging for an event that is actually her birthday party — she's arguing against her own gift. The 'do you people know what fundraising means?' line is high-quotability and captures Moira's event-expertise ego perfectly.

jokes.text: David defending Jocelyn: 'I'm trying to make sense of these flowers... They're seasonal and they tie in all of the other design elements that Jocelyn has come up with.'
jokes.setup: Moira is on the verge of staging a coup against Jocelyn's planning
jokes.punchline: David, who was complaining about the party planning two minutes ago, has now become Jocelyn's champion — his defence of 'seasonal' flowers suggests he's barely masking his own doubts
jokes.explanation: A solid character reversal — David defending what he'd privately disdain — but it's not particularly quotable or original. Functional scene comedy.

jokes.text: Moira announces: 'David, I'm sorry, but it's coup d'etat time.'
jokes.setup: Moira has reached her limit with the planning committee
jokes.punchline: 'Coup d'etat time' — framing the takeover of a birthday party planning session as a political revolution, with full Moira Rose gravitas
jokes.explanation: 'Coup d'etat time' is a four-word sentence that encapsulates everything about Moira Rose. The casual politicisation of party planning, the dramatic framing, and the 'time' suffix (as if there's a scheduled hour for this) make it genuinely memorable. High character integration — this exact phrase construction belongs entirely to Moira. Earns its 8+ score.

jokes.text: David's 'And you are?' to the person who challenged Moira — but delivered to Moira herself as a way of cutting off the confrontation
jokes.setup: Someone tells Moira to 'just sit this one out'
jokes.punchline: David's 'And you are?' — a social snub usually directed outward — seems to be his way of siding against Moira momentarily or deflecting, creating a confused beat
jokes.explanation: Slightly ambiguous from the transcript alone — the joke relies on visual context. As a text beat it's moderate.

jokes.text: David: 'Why don't you just sit this one out then?' — to Moira, when she threatens to leave
jokes.setup: Moira is threatening to leave if she's not given control of the planning
jokes.punchline: David's flat, weary 'Why don't you just sit this one out then?' — the only response that might actually work, delivered without hope
jokes.explanation: A nice David line — the exhausted son who knows exactly the right button to push to redirect his mother. The weariness in 'sit this one out' is funny because David is trying to lose this battle strategically.

jokes.text: Moira threatens to leave: 'I see. Clearly you don't need the help of the most prominent philanthropist in the country. No. Fine. I wish you all the best.' / 'No, seriously, I might leave.'
jokes.setup: Moira has been rebuffed mid-coup
jokes.punchline: Her exit threat escalates hilariously — first the formal speech, then the follow-up 'No, seriously, I might leave' which suggests the first exit threat wasn't taken seriously enough
jokes.explanation: 'No, seriously, I might leave' is the standout line — the second, less formal threat reveals Moira checking whether the first one had its desired effect. The double-exit structure is great comedy architecture. High character integration.

jokes.text: Moira: 'And you are?' — directed at David, her own son, when he challenges her
jokes.setup: David has suggested Moira doesn't need to be involved
jokes.punchline: Moira's ice-cold 'And you are?' — treating her own child as an irrelevant stranger — is the ultimate power move and a perfect character moment
jokes.explanation: Three syllables, devastating. 'And you are?' directed at her own son is the kind of line that defines a character. It's both a power move (dismissing him entirely) and a character reveal (Moira in professional mode doesn't acknowledge personal relationships). Maximum economy, impossible without this specific character, completely earned by her established personality. One of the episode's strongest moments. Comfortably 8+.

jokes.text: Moira: 'No, seriously, I might leave.' — a further ultimatum after already threatening to leave
jokes.setup: Moira has already threatened to walk out, and they've called her bluff
jokes.punchline: Her escalating 'No, seriously, I might leave' — adding 'seriously' as if her earlier threat wasn't serious — reveals she's been bluffing and knows it
jokes.explanation: A good escalation beat. 'No, seriously' inadvertently admitting the previous threat wasn't fully serious is funny and character-specific — Moira can't exit without a dramatic send-off, and she needs to be invited back in.

jokes.text: A visual beat: the barn party setup — we see the party in progress, implying the complicated plan has somehow worked
jokes.setup: David says he thinks she'll be really surprised
jokes.punchline: A pause ('Don't you?') suggests the visual of the barn-turned-party-venue is doing comedic work — the gap between what Moira expects and the barn aesthetic is implied
jokes.explanation: A transitional visual beat — the comedy is implied by context (barn party vs. Moira's expectations) but the transcript doesn't give us enough to score this highly. Functional scene transition.

jokes.text: The $9.99 shopping channel segment — a man earnestly selling cheap jewellery playing on the motel TV while Johnny watches
jokes.setup: Johnny is shown watching TV in the motel room
jokes.punchline: The contrast between Johnny's former wealthy lifestyle and the $9.99 jewellery channel he's now engrossed in is the silent joke
jokes.explanation: A quiet visual character beat — Johnny's fall from wealth made tangible through bad TV. Not a big laugh but a solid piece of environmental comedy.

jokes.text: David's defence of the party planners to Moira: 'Those people are putting a lot of energy into raising money for those kids.'
jokes.setup: Moira is disparaging the volunteers at what is actually her own birthday party
jokes.punchline: David has to earnestly defend a fictional fundraiser — he's now the voice of reason for a cause that doesn't exist
jokes.explanation: The situational irony does good work here. David defending a fake charity with real conviction is a solid laugh.

jokes.text: Johnny tells Moira to 'jump ship, save yourself' — as party planning advice
jokes.setup: Johnny is on the phone with David and apparently advising on how to handle Moira at the planning meeting
jokes.punchline: The nautical metaphor applied to escaping a volunteer committee meeting is disproportionately dramatic
jokes.explanation: A mild joke but the escalation of drama in Johnny's advice is consistent with his character's tendency toward crisis framing.

jokes.text: Johnny slowly walking Moira through the 'fundraiser is actually your birthday party' reveal — Moira says: 'Must you rub it in?'
jokes.setup: Johnny is hinting that the fundraiser is really for her birthday; Moira interprets each hint as an insult about her age
jokes.punchline: 'Must you rub it in?' — Moira thinks Johnny is cruelly drawing attention to her birthday (and therefore her age), when he's actually trying to tell her she's getting a party
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection. The double meaning of 'your birthday' — as both the reason for celebration and the age Moira is sensitive about — creates a perfect comic miscommunication. Moira's vanity and sensitivity about age are well-integrated here.

jokes.text: Johnny finally says plainly: 'The fundraiser isn't real. It's not a real thing.' Moira: 'Oh! You're planning a birthday thing that I'm not supposed to know about.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has been gradually, then bluntly, revealing the birthday surprise plan
jokes.punchline: Moira's sudden perfect comprehension — 'a birthday thing I'm not supposed to know about' — delivered as a discovery rather than a disappointment
jokes.explanation: The reveal lands well because Moira's comprehension arrives at exactly the moment Johnny has abandoned all subtlety. The 'Mmm-mmm' and 'In a barn?' that follow (joke #63) make this a setup for something better, but as a standalone it works.

jokes.text: After Moira figures out the birthday party, she adds: 'In a barn?'
jokes.setup: Moira has just deduced she's getting a surprise party
jokes.punchline: 'In a barn?' — one of the Rose family's greatest humiliations, expressed in two words with perfect aristocratic horror
jokes.explanation: Two words. Maximum character expression. Moira Rose — former socialite, board member of profitable non-profits, person who made Hillary shake — is getting a surprise birthday party in a barn. The economy is extraordinary: 'In a barn?' does everything. It's horror, acceptance, and aristocratic indignation compressed into the smallest possible space. Earns high scores.

jokes.text: The candles argument: 'We can't afford the candles. They're $2.49.' / 'They don't just come with the cake?' / 'No, apparently not.'
jokes.setup: Johnny and Alexis are at the bakery, having retrieved the cake
jokes.punchline: A family that once had a 300-foot yacht is debating whether to spend $2.49 on birthday candles
jokes.explanation: A fish-out-of-water reversal played gently. Alexis's genuine confusion about candle economics ('they don't just come with the cake?') suggests someone who has never purchased a birthday cake in a shop. The $2.49 figure makes it more pathetic by being so small.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Not if you just take them.' David: 'Well, that would be low-bottom behaviour, wouldn't it?'
jokes.setup: David has said they have to pay for the candles if there's a price tag
jokes.punchline: David's 'low-bottom behaviour' — a very specific, slightly clinical phrase applied to the concept of stealing birthday candles — is funny because of its disproportionate moral seriousness
jokes.explanation: 'Low-bottom behaviour' is a David Rose phrase — formal, slightly therapeutic in register, applied to something trivial. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Oh, my God, I raised a thief. Go wait in the car!'
jokes.setup: Alexis has just admitted to stealing sunglasses from a gas station
jokes.punchline: Johnny's parent-mode kicks in — 'I raised a thief' — followed by sending a grown woman to 'wait in the car' like a child
jokes.explanation: 'Go wait in the car' directed at an adult daughter is a perfect comedy beat. Johnny's reversion to old-fashioned paternal discipline — sending a grown woman to the car — is funny in its anachronism and his genuine indignation.

jokes.text: Johnny in the bakery, clearly overcome by the smell, filling awkward silence with '...so delicious in here'
jokes.setup: Johnny identifies himself to pick up the cake. There's a pause.
jokes.punchline: Johnny filling the silence by commenting on the smell — the social awkwardness of a man who used to have staff do everything, now personally picking up a birthday cake
jokes.explanation: A quiet character beat — Johnny's discomfort with doing ordinary errands reads as gentle comedy. Not a strong laugh but tonally consistent.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Like your father the day I married him.' — describing the perfect cake
jokes.setup: Moira is admiring the finished birthday cake
jokes.punchline: She compares it to Johnny on their wedding day — a sweet compliment, but the framing ('perfect, like your father') is so theatrical and Moira-esque that it reads as comedy
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: it's genuinely sweet (she's complimenting Johnny) and it's also very Moira — finding the theatrical angle in everything. The delivery implied by the text suggests she's quite pleased with herself for the comparison.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I think you're overselling it just a bit.' Moira: 'Well, I don't see any stragglers, so shall we go in? I think we're fashionably late enough.' Johnny: 'There's nothing fashionable about this, but...'
jokes.setup: Moira is treating her own surprise party as a glamorous event to be fashionably late to
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'there's nothing fashionable about this' — the Rose family attending a surprise party in a small-town barn, pretending it's a gala
jokes.explanation: A nice contrast beat — Moira in full socialite mode, Johnny grounded in the absurdity of their situation. Good character work with decent economy.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'There's nothing fashionable about this, but... After you, my dear.'
jokes.setup: Moira says they're 'fashionably late enough'
jokes.punchline: Johnny acknowledges there's nothing fashionable about any of this, then pivots immediately to gallantry — his warm love for her cutting through the realism
jokes.explanation: A warm beat that works as gentle comedy and character intimacy simultaneously. Johnny's combination of bluntness and affection in a single breath is what makes him a great character. The comedy is soft here — this is more heart than funny.

jokes.text: Moira enters the party and asks: 'Where are all the patrons?' — still believing it's the fundraiser
jokes.setup: They've arrived at the surprise party, which Moira still thinks is Jocelyn's fundraiser
jokes.punchline: 'Patrons' — the word she uses for what she expected to be a fundraiser crowd, in a barn in Schitt's Creek
jokes.explanation: 'Patrons' is a perfectly Moira word to use for party guests — it signals both her expectations and her class conditioning. Good setup for the surprise reveal.

jokes.text: After the surprise: 'What! There's no fundraiser? You're just here to celebrate me?'
jokes.setup: The surprise is revealed — everyone yells 'Surprise!'
jokes.punchline: Moira's response has two parts: genuine shock at there being no fundraiser, then the pivot to 'you're just here to celebrate me?' — as if this is somehow less than what she expected and also exactly right
jokes.explanation: 'You're just here to celebrate me?' — the 'just' does enormous work. It implies that mere celebration of Moira Rose is somehow insufficient, while also being exactly what she wants. Character integration at its finest.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, never in the history of surprises has one been so delightfully blindsided.'
jokes.setup: Moira has just been 'surprised' by a party she helped plan and knew about
jokes.punchline: She delivers the most theatrical possible reaction — 'never in the history of surprises' — for a surprise she orchestrated herself
jokes.explanation: Moira commits completely to the performance of surprise even though she knows everything. 'Never in the history of surprises' is a grand, sweeping claim that is both perfectly Moira and darkly ironic. High quotability and character integration.

jokes.text: Someone at the party immediately asks: 'Okay, who told her?'
jokes.setup: Moira has expressed that she knew about the party
jokes.punchline: The automatic assumption that someone leaked the surprise — not that Moira figured it out on her own (despite helping plan it)
jokes.explanation: A solid button on the surprise reveal. The assumption of a leak rather than the obvious explanation (she helped plan it) is a good comic beat.

jokes.text: Stevie and David at the party: 'And you got your cake.' / 'Yeah, remember this cake? I'd asked you to help me get it.'
jokes.setup: The cake has been successfully retrieved after the whole ordeal
jokes.punchline: David's passive-aggressive reminder that Stevie refused to help him get it — but delivered at a party, in a moment of supposed reconciliation
jokes.explanation: A callback that works as character comedy — David can't let it go, even here. Mild but consistent.

jokes.text: Stevie begins explaining her earlier behaviour: 'I think it's just that this whole friend...' — David interrupts: 'It's that lady time, huh?'
jokes.setup: Stevie is trying to have a genuine conversation about the difficulties of their evolving friendship
jokes.punchline: David interrupts with 'lady time' — a completely misread, sexist assumption that derails the moment
jokes.explanation: David's 'lady time' interruption is uncomfortable in exactly the right way — it's the wrong read, it derails a sincere moment, and it says a lot about David's emotional avoidance. The cringe is intentional and character-specific.

jokes.text: Stevie's explanation of why she kept David at arm's length: 'I may have built it a little high'
jokes.setup: Stevie is explaining that building a barrier in their friendship was a mistake
jokes.punchline: The masterful understatement — she didn't just build the barrier, she built it 'a little high' — as dry and restrained a confession as possible
jokes.explanation: Stevie's emotional register — maximum understatement — is perfectly deployed here. 'A little high' is exactly how Stevie would admit to a significant emotional mistake. Great character integration and economy.

jokes.text: David asks Stevie to dance; she says she 'normally only slow dances with strangers, at bars... after I've had a few.' Then agrees.
jokes.setup: David asks if two friends can dance as a way of marking their reconciliation
jokes.punchline: Stevie's conditions for slow dancing — strangers, bars, alcohol — establishes her as deeply uncomfortable with emotional intimacy even in small ways
jokes.explanation: A warm, funny character moment. Stevie's rules for slow dancing are perfectly in character — her emotional avoidance made concrete in very specific social conditions. The 'I can make an exception' that follows makes it also sweet.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Um, did you have to kill a man to get it, or...' — about Mutt's suit
jokes.setup: Mutt is wearing a suit, which is unusual for him
jokes.punchline: Alexis's casual suggestion that the suit may have been obtained through violence — a half-joke, half-flirt
jokes.explanation: A neat little dark-subversive flirt. Alexis using murder as an icebreaker is both absurd and entirely in her register.

jokes.text: Mutt: 'I did. It took me a couple to find one in my size.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has half-jokingly asked if he killed someone for the suit
jokes.punchline: Mutt plays it completely straight — he killed multiple people, specifically because of the sizing challenge
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan escalation. The callback to multiple murders, motivated entirely by practical suit-fitting concerns, is genuinely funny and well-constructed. 'It took me a couple to find one in my size' is highly quotable and earns its scores. The sizing detail is what elevates it from decent to excellent.

jokes.text: Mutt: 'I wore this thing to prom, like, 1,000 years ago, but it did bring me some luck that night, so I wore it here tonight.'
jokes.setup: Mutt is explaining why he's wearing the suit
jokes.punchline: The implication that prom-suit luck might transfer to tonight — a romantic/superstitious claim that's also a transparent flirtation with Alexis
jokes.explanation: A nice piece of character flirtation. The prom-luck superstition is endearing and the implication is clear. '1,000 years ago' is a good hyperbole.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'You were planning on getting lucky tonight?'
jokes.setup: Mutt has just implied the suit brought him 'luck' at prom
jokes.punchline: Alexis pounces on 'luck' with its romantic/sexual double meaning — enjoying the fact that she's the intended recipient of this luck
jokes.explanation: A clean double-entendre beat. Alexis's enjoyment of catching the implication is good character work.

jokes.text: Ted shows up at the party despite supposedly being at the conference: 'Well, I had someone take my last seminar. I didn't wanna miss this.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is flirting with Mutt, about to dance — then Ted appears
jokes.punchline: Ted's sweet arrival completely disrupts the Mutt/Alexis moment — comedy through perfect timing and the oblivious wholesomeness of Ted
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the timing — Mutt and Alexis were about to have a moment and Ted's earnest arrival deflates it perfectly. His explanation is so genuinely sweet it becomes darkly funny given what was about to happen.

jokes.text: Ted to Mutt, about the suit: 'Great suit, bud.' — Mutt: 'Thanks.' — then Alexis immediately to Mutt: 'Killed somebody for it.'
jokes.setup: Ted compliments the suit; Mutt has already explained the murder origin story
jokes.punchline: Alexis delivers the murder callback for Mutt — now as a breezy party fact rather than a dark joke
jokes.explanation: An excellent callback execution. Alexis casually telling Ted that Mutt killed someone for the suit — with zero further explanation — is both funny in itself and reveals that she and Mutt have already established their own comedic language. The economy is excellent and the callback reward is satisfying.

jokes.text: Ted to Mutt: 'Great suit, bud.' / Mutt: 'Thanks.' — followed by Alexis saying 'Babe, I thought you had that...' / Mutt: 'Killed somebody for it.'
jokes.setup: Ted arrives and compliments Mutt's suit
jokes.punchline: Mutt reprises the 'killed somebody for it' bit for Ted — who has no context — making it even funnier as a non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: A great callback deployment. 'Killed somebody for it' lands even harder the second time because Ted has zero context — to him, it's just a completely insane response to a suit compliment. The comedy of Mutt repeating his bit to a confused new audience, with Alexis knowing exactly where this came from, is structurally satisfying. Economy is excellent — four words carry enormous funny weight.

jokes.text: Moira tells Johnny: 'I love my party, John. But please don't ever do it again.'
jokes.setup: The party is a success; this is Moira's thank you
jokes.punchline: 'But please don't ever do it again' — Moira's gratitude has a built-in veto on future gestures of this kind
jokes.explanation: A perfect episode button from Moira. The line manages to be simultaneously touching and funny — she loved it but the experience of a barn party in Schitt's Creek is something she is not willing to repeat. 'But please don't ever do it again' distils Moira Rose's entire character: grateful, dignified, impossible. High marks across the board.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Well, the surprise was on me, 'cause this town cleaned itself up pretty well.'
jokes.setup: Moira has asked that this never happen again
jokes.punchline: Johnny's grudging admission that Schitt's Creek pulled off a decent party — the 'cleaned itself up' phrasing suggesting a lowered baseline he's surprised was met
jokes.explanation: A warm, character-specific observation that also functions as a thematic note for the episode. 'Cleaned itself up' implies the town is capable of self-improvement even if just situationally — and Johnny's surprise is the fish-out-of-water arc beginning to soften. Good episode closure comedy.

tmdb_overview: Johnny tries to arrange a surprise birthday party for Moira. Things backfire when Moira, unwittingly, takes over the planning of her own party.
insight: Character-driven cringe carries a slower episode through escalating social disasters.
